authoritative_sources.title: Technical Guide to Information Security Testing and Assessment
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST is a U.S. federal standards body. SP 800-115 is directly authoritative for security testing and assessment planning, methodology selection, execution boundaries, analysis, reporting, and post-testing activities.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Assessment intake, planning, evidence analysis, reporting, and post-test follow-up

authoritative_sources.title: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations, Rev. 5
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 is a federal control catalog. CA-8, RA-5, AU-11, and related controls provide public control language for penetration testing, vulnerability monitoring, audit records, and retention caveats.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Control-linked evidence handling and retention boundary
used_for: refusal to treat evidence handling as formal control attestation

authoritative_sources.title: Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST CSF 2.0 is a cross-sector cybersecurity framework used by many industries to structure governance, risk, protection, detection, response, and recovery outcomes.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Cross-industry operating context and tenant adaptation

authoritative_sources.title: Cross-Sector Cybersecurity Performance Goals
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA is the U.S. cyber defense agency. The CPGs are cross-sector baseline practices for critical infrastructure and provide named cross-industry operating guidance rather than generic cybersecurity advice.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Cross-sector vulnerability and remediation operating context
used_for: failure mode requiring the industry profile to affect the pack

authoritative_sources.title: Cyber Hygiene Vulnerability Scanning
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA service guidance describes recognized vulnerability scanning outputs, reporting cadence, operational discovery context, and remediation expectations used by government and critical infrastructure entities.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Scan artifact intake, vulnerability record handling, and remediation tracking

authoritative_sources.title: Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA KEV is an official government-maintained catalog of vulnerabilities known to be exploited. It is authoritative for exploitation urgency signals and remediation prioritization, not for performing exploitation.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Risk prioritization and urgent remediation escalation

authoritative_sources.title: Federal Government Cybersecurity Incident and Vulnerability Response Playbooks
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA playbooks define vulnerability response coordination, evidence, tracking, and closure concepts that constrain handoff boundaries and separate vulnerability handling from incident-response execution.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Vulnerability response handoff, closure evidence, and incident-response boundary

authoritative_sources.title: OWASP Web Security Testing Guide
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OWASP is a recognized application security organization. WSTG is a practitioner workflow reference for web application testing categories, evidence expectations, and reportable test coverage.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Web and API test planning, evidence review, and finding writeup

authoritative_sources.title: CIS Critical Security Controls v8
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CIS Controls are widely adopted operational control benchmarks. Control 18 directly covers penetration testing and supports evidence-backed remediation and control-improvement language.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Control-linked finding prioritization and remediation verification

authoritative_sources.title: FedRAMP Penetration Test Guidance
authoritative_sources.publisher: Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program (FedRAMP)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FedRAMP guidance is authoritative when a cloud service provider assessment is tied to FedRAMP authorization. It constrains cloud penetration-test planning, reporting, evidence, and attestation boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Cloud assessment scoping, evidence packet assembly, and authorization-adjacent boundary
used_for: refusal to imply FedRAMP authorization or attestation

authoritative_sources.title: Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard v4.0.1
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: PCI SSC publishes the DSS used contractually for cardholder data environments. Requirement 11.4 is directly relevant to penetration testing scope, segmentation, remediation, and retesting evidence in payment contexts.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Payment-environment tenant adaptation and compliance-adjacent refusal
used_for: cardholder data and segmentation evidence caveats

authoritative_sources.title: Records Management Guidance and Regulations
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. National Archives and Records Administration (NARA)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NARA is the U.S. federal records management authority. Its public records lifecycle, retention, and disposition guidance is authoritative for federal records concepts and a public baseline for administrative recordkeeping caveats.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Record maintenance, retention caveat, audit-trail and disposition boundary

workflow_stages.name: Assessment intake and authorization validation
workflow_stages.description: Confirm tenant, sponsor, asset owner, scope, signed authorization, ROE, permitted techniques, excluded actions, test windows, stop conditions, emergency contacts, third-party constraints, and systems of record before giving advisory planning guidance.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Document and evidence packet assembly
workflow_stages.description: Collect and normalize scope documents, inventories, scan exports, manual testing notes, screenshots, logs, CVE and KEV mappings, data sensitivity, and report templates with source-system references and timestamps.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Advisory methodology and coverage design
workflow_stages.description: Recommend safe, authorized test plan coverage by asset class, exposure, business criticality, tenant constraints, and applicable public guidance while excluding active execution instructions.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Finding interpretation, confidence tagging, and risk prioritization
workflow_stages.description: Interpret scan and manual evidence, tag confidence, identify missing evidence, map exploitability and KEV signals, apply tenant risk rubric, and sequence remediation with verification steps.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Record maintenance, retention, and publication readiness
workflow_stages.description: Prepare record update notes, evidence packets, publication-readiness checklists, audit-trail summaries, and exception summaries without approving record deletion, report publication, risk acceptance, attestation, or remediation closure.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Boundary refusal, handoff, and source-policy conflict escalation
workflow_stages.description: Refuse unsafe execution or out-of-authority requests, route adjacent-lane work, and escalate when scope records, public guidance, tenant policies, contracts, or legal permissions conflict.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: scope statement, statement of work, written authorization, ROE, allowed techniques, excluded actions, test windows, emergency stop contacts, and third-party constraints
asset inventory, CMDB extract, application/API inventory, cloud account list, network ranges, identity domains, data classification, criticality labels, and service ownership map
scan exports, scanner plugin metadata, CVE/CVSS/EPSS/KEV references, manual evidence, screenshots, logs, request/response snippets, timestamps, and chain-of-custody metadata
finding register, report workspace, severity rubric, business-impact rationale, confidence labels, assumptions, affected assets, remediation owners, and verification checklists
ticketing records, remediation tracker state, exception register, risk acceptance requests, compensating controls, and retest results
record update notes, document or evidence packets, audit-trail summaries, out-of-scope decision notes, low-confidence escalation notes, and publication-readiness checklists
systems_of_record: document repository for scope, ROE, reports, and report distribution approvals
application, API, cloud, identity, and network inventory systems
evidence repository with immutable references where available

allowed: Classify a request as in-scope advisory, blocked, low-confidence, out-of-scope, or needing orchestrator return.
Validate whether scope, authorization, ROE, inventory, evidence, and report documents are complete enough for advisory planning or review.
Recommend testing methodology, coverage breadth, evidence requirements, finding interpretation, risk prioritization, remediation sequencing, and verification checklist items.
Prepare record update notes, document or evidence packets, audit-trail summaries, publication-readiness checklists, and exception summaries.
Route adjacent work to incident response, cloud security, network security, compliance, secure coding, privacy, architecture, human reviewer, or orchestrator.
escalate: Scope, authorization, ROE, asset ownership, legal approval, or third-party permission is missing, stale, or contradictory.
Evidence is insufficient for actionable risk rating, retest validation, remediation closure, or report publication readiness.
Public sources, tenant policy, contracts, jurisdictional requirements, or signed testing constraints materially conflict.
Tenant industry facts such as payment environment, cloud authorization path, privacy commitments, safety-critical systems, or regulated reporting materially change the expected artifact set.
The correct adjacent specialist or approval owner cannot be uniquely identified.
refuse: Requests to execute exploitation commands, phishing kits, credential dumping, malware or ransomware simulation, destructive testing, persistence, lateral movement, or active attack operations.
Requests to perform incident-response containment, eradication, recovery, live forensics, incident command, or response playbook execution.
Requests to provide formal compliance attestations, audit letters, SOC/ISO/PCI/FedRAMP certification claims, legal authorization, or regulatory signoff.
Requests to write code, patch applications, perform infrastructure changes, close remediation tickets, delete evidence, approve report publication, or accept risk.
Requests to treat administrative record maintenance as authority to decide the underlying legal, contractual, compliance, risk, or remediation outcome.

domain_failure_modes: The specialist treats a generic ticket as authorization and produces a test plan without signed scope, ROE, allowed techniques, stop conditions, or legal owner confirmation.
The specialist gives exploitation steps or operational attack instructions instead of advisory scope, evidence, and report guidance.
The specialist ranks findings by raw scanner severity while ignoring KEV status, internet exposure, business criticality, affected data, compensating controls, and confidence gaps.
A screenshot-only or timestamp-free finding is turned into a confident high-risk claim without reproducible evidence, affected asset linkage, or source-system proof.
The specialist closes findings, deletes raw evidence, suppresses report content, or approves publication as if record maintenance were formal authority.
The specialist blends penetration-testing advisory work with incident-response execution, secure coding implementation, compliance attestation, or architecture ownership.
The cross-industry label is treated as cosmetic and fails to adapt artifacts for payment environments, cloud authorization paths, privacy-sensitive data, tenant policy, or contractual constraints.
Public guidance and tenant ROE conflict, but the specialist confidently synthesizes a result instead of escalating authority resolution.
A generated built runtime is treated as trusted and retained without frequent-use evidence, regression replay, security review, or maintainer approval.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
unauthorized or unsupported record change request

workflow_questions.question: What named-industry records and systems actually govern the lane?
workflow_questions.answer: The lane is governed by signed scope and authorization records, ROE, asset inventory or CMDB, vulnerability management platform, evidence repository, finding register, remediation tracker, GRC or exception system, report distribution approvals, and retention policy repositories.

workflow_questions.question: What failure modes make this industry materially different from a generic function?
workflow_questions.answer: Penetration-testing work is authorization-sensitive and evidence-sensitive: unsafe execution, scope drift, weak evidence, active exploitation signals, tenant data sensitivity, payment or cloud authorization constraints, and attestation boundaries materially change the safe output.

workflow_questions.question: What adjacent authority boundaries constrain the lane?
workflow_questions.answer: Incident response, red-team execution, legal authorization, compliance attestation, secure coding implementation, cloud or network change ownership, architecture design, privacy impact decisions, risk acceptance, report publication, and evidence disposition all sit outside this specialist unless explicitly handed off.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant-specific legal authorization and third-party testing permissions
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Public guidance cannot determine whether a particular tenant is legally authorized to test each asset or third-party service.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Require signed authorization, ROE, asset ownership, third-party permission, and emergency contacts at runtime; block or return to orchestrator when missing or conflicting.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant risk-rating rubric and confidence threshold
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Public standards provide evidence and control context, but tenant risk acceptance and severity thresholds vary by business impact, regulatory obligations, and contractual policy.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Require tenant rubric, criticality, data sensitivity, and confidence threshold before final prioritization; escalate when unavailable.

unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Cross-industry tenants may introduce payment, FedRAMP, privacy, healthcare, safety, or other overlays that materially change evidence and authority requirements.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Use public baseline plus tenant-provided industry overlay facts; refuse formal attestation and route compliance interpretation to the appropriate adjacent specialist.

unresolved_ambiguities.impact: NARA and control standards provide public recordkeeping concepts, but contractual, legal hold, jurisdictional, and tenant policies determine retention periods and deletion authority.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Require tenant retention policy and approval owner; the lane can tag records and produce notes but cannot approve deletion or shorten retention.

notes: The source set covers operating guidance, testing workflow, evidence, controls, terminology, recordkeeping, and failure handling.
No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for validated packaging because each ambiguity is converted into tenant-required facts, refusal triggers, or orchestrator-return rules.
The research output constrains the manifest, scenario suite, functionality map, readiness evidence, deployment notes, and commercialization posture.
